message: People with learning disabilities can have trouble with reading, writing, and/or mathematics, and sometimes language. They have trouble more than other people, which can affect achievement at school.  Everyone’s brain is a bit different and for people with a learning disability there are processes in the brain, that don’t work together as well and as a result, can affect performance.
To do tasks, we rely on many different brain processes, like memory and language.  These processes can affect how we read, do math, and write.
When you say my brain processes don't work as well, it makes it sound like I'm stupid.

The big thing to remember is that a learning disability is not related to a person's intelligence but rather how they acquire and use information.  There are very smart people out there who have learning disabilities.
With my learning challenges, maybe postsecondary isn't for me.
Many people with learning disabilities complete postsecondary and go on to have amazing careers! It's just that they may need different approaches and strategies for learning when they are there.
I don't think it's fair that I have to deal with this and no one else does.

Actually, you are not alone. It is estimated that one in 10 Canadians have a learning disability, and that more children in this country have a learning disability than all other types of disabilities combined. Schools are aware of learning disabilities and have an obligation to support your learning.
